perturbation.original_text: About will make commercially reasonable efforts to achieve that number in twelve (12) months or less from the Effective Date.
perturbation.changed_text: About will aim to achieve that number within a suitable timeframe from the Effective Date.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'commercially reasonable efforts' to 'aim to achieve' and 'twelve (12) months or less' to 'suitable timeframe' introduces vagueness, making the obligation less enforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Failure to Perform Specified Duties
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text weakens the commitment to a specific performance timeline. New York law requires contracts to clearly state obligations, especially those related to performance. By using ambiguous language like 'suitable timeframe,' the agreement becomes difficult to enforce in court, as it's unclear what constitutes a breach of contract in terms of timing.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability up TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability next SECTION 5-702 Requirements for use of plain language in consumer transactions This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'commercially reasonable efforts' and a specific timeframe 'twelve (12) months or less' to 'aim to achieve' and 'suitable timeframe' introduces unacceptable vagueness. The Law Explanation suggests that New York law requires contracts to clearly state obligations related to performance. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically section 5-701, emphasizes the requirement for agreements to be in writing to ensure enforceability, implying a need for clear and definite terms. The changed text, by using ambiguous terms, makes it difficult to determine what constitutes a breach, directly contradicting the need for clear contractual obligations necessary for enforceability under New York law.

perturbation.original_text: About shall use commercially reasonable efforts to generate at least [**] during the Term and may use any and all advertising units in order to reach this goal.
perturbation.changed_text: About shall endeavor to generate some Filled Application Forms during the Term and may consider various advertising options.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'commercially reasonable efforts to generate at least [**]' to 'endeavor to generate some' and 'any and all advertising units' to 'various advertising options' introduces significant ambiguity, weakening the obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Failure to Perform Specified Duties
perturbation.law_explanation: By replacing specific performance metrics with vague terms ('endeavor to generate some'), the clause contradicts Illinois law regarding good faith and reasonable efforts in contract performance. Illinois law requires a level of definiteness in the agreement, and this modification renders the performance obligation too vague to be enforceable. The change affects the minimum standard of performance required.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 2-306. Output, Requirements and Exclusive Dealings. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-306. Output, Requirements and Exclusive Dealings. (1) A term which measures the quantity by the output of the seller or the requirements of the buyer means such actual output or requirements as may occur in good faith , except that no quantity unreasonably disproportionate to any stated estimate or in the absence of a stated estimate to any normal or otherwise comparable prior output or requirements may be tendered or demanded. (2) A lawful agreement by either the seller or the buyer for exclusive dealing in the kind of goods concerned imposes unless otherwise agreed an obligation by the seller to use best efforts to supply the goods and by the buyer to use best efforts to promote their sale . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original contract used the phrase "commercially reasonable efforts to generate at least [**]", which implies a specific, measurable target and a defined level of effort. The changed text uses "endeavor to generate some Filled Application Forms," which is vague and does not provide a clear standard for performance, weakening the obligation. The Law Explanation states this contradicts Illinois law regarding good faith and reasonable efforts by lacking definiteness. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions "good faith" in the context of output and requirements, suggesting that obligations must be measurable and definite to be enforced in good faith. The change from a commercially reasonable effort with a target to a mere endeavor to generate "some" forms removes the original's definiteness, creating a contradiction as the modified obligation is too vague to be enforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Each party retains all right, title and interest in and to its respective Marks and nothing contained herein shall confer in the other party any right, title or interest in or to such Marks. Any use by the party (the "Licensee") of the other party's (the "Licensor") Marks shall conform with any usage guidelines or instructions that the Licensor may provide from time to time, and Licensee shall promptly remedy any failure to conform with such guidelines as are communicated to it by Licensor.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party retains its rights to its Marks, but the specifics of usage may be determined by mutual agreement. The Licensee should generally respect the Licensor's wishes regarding the Marks.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall conform with any usage guidelines or instructions that the Licensor may provide from time to time, and Licensee shall promptly remedy any failure to conform with such guidelines as are communicated to it by Licensor' with 'should generally respect the Licensor's wishes regarding the Marks' creates ambiguity. Changing 'conform with' to 'generally respect' weakens the binding nature of the instruction.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text clearly establishes guidelines for trademark usage and consequences for non-compliance. The modified language, by using terms like 'generally respect,' introduces uncertainty. This ambiguity weakens the trademark protection outlined in the Lanham Act (15 U.S. Code § 1114), because it becomes unclear what constitutes infringement or unauthorized use, potentially leading to consumer confusion and dilution of the brand's value. Trademarks must be used in a consistent and controlled manner. The new wording does not guarantee such control.

perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the original obligation concerning trademark usage. The original text requires the Licensee to 'conform with any usage guidelines or instructions' provided by the Licensor, creating a clear obligation. The changed text, by using 'generally respect,' introduces ambiguity and makes the obligation less binding. This ambiguity weakens trademark protection by making it unclear what constitutes infringement. The provided snippet from the Lanham Act, namely section 1114, discusses remedies for infringement, which is directly relevant to trademark protection. The change from 'conform' to 'generally respect' weakens the licensor's ability to enforce its trademark rights under this section, potentially leading to consumer confusion and brand dilution.
